articles.label: Introduction
articles.title: Vanrot
articles.summary: Vanrot is a small frontend framework with compiler-known templates, signals, route ownership, source-owned UI, and a documentation-first development path.

sections.title: What Vanrot Gives You
sections.body: Vanrot combines a small runtime, compiler role files, a Vite plugin, a typed router, a guided CLI, and source-owned UI primitives.

sections.title: What Exists Now
sections.body: The implemented surface includes runtime signals, compiled components and pages, scoped CSS, config loading, CLI commands, production router behavior, October tokens, vanrotstyles, and Phase 16B UI primitives.

articles.label: Installation
articles.title: Installation
articles.summary: Create or run a Vanrot app through the CLI and workspace package graph.

sections.title: Create A Project
sections.body: Use vr create <name> for generated apps. In this monorepo, workspace links are used until package publishing is complete.

sections.title: Scripts
sections.body: Generated apps use vr dev, vr build, vr test, and vr doctor so project commands stay conventional.

articles.label: Project Structure
articles.title: Project Structure
articles.summary: Vanrot keeps source roles explicit with TypeScript, HTML, and CSS side by side.

sections.title: Role Files
sections.body: Components use .component.ts/html/css, pages use .page.ts/html/css, layouts use .layout.ts/html/css, and UI primitives use role suffixes such as .button.ts/html/css.

sections.title: Source Root
sections.body: vanrot.config.ts owns the source root. The default is src.

articles.label: Runtime
articles.title: Runtime
articles.summary: The runtime owns signals, computed values, effects, lifecycle hooks, inputs, and mounting.

sections.title: Signals
sections.body: Use signal(), computed(), effect(), batch(), and untrack() for reactive state. The runtime stays small and is protected by the size budget.

sections.title: Lifecycle
sections.body: Use onMount() and onDestroy() for component lifecycle work. Cleanup scopes are automatic through mount and generated code.

articles.label: Compiler
articles.title: Compiler
articles.summary: The compiler turns role files into mounted DOM with scoped CSS, diagnostics, source maps, control flow, child components, slots, and UI primitive lowering.

sections.title: Templates
sections.body: Vanrot templates support interpolation, property binding, event binding, @if, @for, child components, slots, route links, and supported vr-* UI primitives.

sections.title: Diagnostics
sections.body: Compiler diagnostics include stable codes, source locations, suggestions, and docs paths.

articles.label: Vite Plugin
articles.title: Vite Plugin
articles.summary: The Vite plugin compiles Vanrot role files in dev and build, bridges diagnostics, watches sibling files, and emits source maps.

sections.title: Usage
sections.body: Add vanrot() to Vite plugins. The plugin reads vanrot.config.ts and uses the configured source root.

sections.title: HMR
sections.body: HTML and CSS sibling edits invalidate the owner role module so development stays predictable.

articles.label: CLI
articles.title: CLI
articles.summary: The CLI provides project creation, generation, UI primitive add flows, dev/build/test wrappers, doctor, map, config maintenance, and AI-readable local context.

sections.title: Commands
sections.body: Current commands are create, generate, add, config, doctor, map, init-ai, ai, dev, build, and test.

sections.title: Output
sections.body: Phase 14 made CLI output grouped, readable, colored where supported, and structured for selected read commands.

articles.label: Configuration
articles.title: Configuration
articles.summary: vanrot.config.ts is the typed source of truth for source roots, dev server port, UI flavor, UI style mode, and future package domains.

sections.title: Defaults
sections.body: The default source root is src, the default dev server port is 1010, the default UI flavor is october, and the default UI style mode is vanrotstyles.

sections.title: Maintenance
sections.body: Use vr config migrate and vr config recover to create or repair canonical config.

articles.label: Routing
articles.title: Routing
articles.summary: The router owns route refs, nested layouts, params, query strings, redirects, guards, active links, breadcrumbs, preloading, and keepAlive.

sections.title: Define Routes
sections.body: Use createRoutes() and defineRoutes({ ... }) so paths, labels, navigation metadata, breadcrumbs, and route refs live in one place.

sections.title: Performance
sections.body: Routes can opt into intent preloading and same-day keepAlive policies.

articles.label: October
articles.title: UI October
articles.summary: October is Vanrot's dark-first, light-capable UI foundation with source-owned primitives, tokens, and vanrotstyles.

sections.title: Source Ownership
sections.body: Vanrot UI follows a developer-owned model. Primitives are readable source files and compiler-lowered semantic tags, not opaque black boxes.

sections.title: Current Primitives
sections.body: Phase 16B includes button, card, badge, avatar, alert, loader, skeleton, and separator.

articles.label: Theming
articles.title: Theming
articles.summary: Vanrot themes use CSS custom properties for colors, surfaces, radius, shadows, typography, motion, and z-index layers.

sections.title: Dark First, Light Capable
sections.body: October defaults to a dark-first theme and supports data-theme="light" for light mode.

sections.title: Typography
sections.body: The default identity uses Geist for text and JetBrains Mono for numeric UI.

articles.label: vanrotstyles
articles.title: vanrotstyles
articles.summary: vanrotstyles.css is Vanrot's first-party utility CSS layer with unprefixed utility classes.

sections.title: Usage
sections.body: Use ui.styles: 'vanrotstyles' to use the first-party utility layer. Use 'tailwind' or 'none' when the app owns another style path.

sections.title: Utilities
sections.body: Utilities cover display, flex, grid, spacing, sizing, typography, surfaces, borders, radius, shadows, motion, overflow, z-index, and accessibility helpers.

articles.label: Testing
articles.title: Testing
articles.summary: Vanrot testing currently provides component test helpers and will expand after the production UI and site base are in place.

sections.title: Current Helper
sections.body: testComponent(...) mounts a component through the runtime and exposes a small screen helper.

sections.title: Future Scope
sections.body: Phase 18 owns page tests, router workflows, accessibility assertions, async helpers, fake timers, and generator-wide test output.

articles.label: Examples
articles.title: Examples
articles.summary: Examples show practical Vanrot usage without hiding the source files.

sections.title: Counter
sections.body: examples/counter demonstrates runtime state, compiler role files, Vite integration, and build verification.

sections.title: Vanrot Site
sections.body: apps/vanrot-site becomes the framework's living documentation example.

articles.label: Conventions
articles.title: Conventions
articles.summary: Vanrot conventions keep projects readable for humans and AI tools.

sections.title: Role Suffixes
sections.body: Use .component, .page, .layout, .dialog, .widget, .form, and UI primitive role suffixes for Vanrot-owned files.

sections.title: Source Of Truth
sections.body: Avoid reused strings for routes, command names, diagnostics, paths, labels, generated copy, and file suffixes.

articles.label: Status
articles.title: Reference Status
articles.summary: The reference section shows what is available now, demo-capable, in progress, planned, or deferred.

sections.title: Production Phases
sections.body: Phases 11 through 15 are complete. Phase 16A and 16B are complete slices. Phase 16C creates this learning site base.

sections.title: Final Audit
sections.body: Phase 24 remains the final documentation and public web presence audit after the feature surface stabilizes.

primitiveDocs.title: Button
primitiveDocs.summary: Use buttons for actions and command triggers.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Buttons lower to native button elements and preserve focus, disabled, keyboard, and type behavior.

primitiveDocs.title: Card
primitiveDocs.summary: Use cards for grouped content surfaces.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Cards lower to article-like surfaces and keep child content readable.

primitiveDocs.title: Badge
primitiveDocs.summary: Use badges for short status, category, or metadata labels.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Badge examples should not rely on color alone for meaning.

primitiveDocs.title: Avatar
primitiveDocs.summary: Use avatars for person, tenant, team, or entity identity.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Avatar content should include accessible names when it represents a person or entity.

primitiveDocs.title: Alert
primitiveDocs.summary: Use alerts for persistent inline feedback.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Alerts use severity-aware semantics without making every note interruptive.

primitiveDocs.title: Loader
primitiveDocs.summary: Use loaders for pending activity.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Decorative loaders are hidden by default unless labelled.

primitiveDocs.title: Skeleton
primitiveDocs.summary: Use skeletons to preserve layout while content loads.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Skeletons are decorative by default and should not replace real loading labels.

primitiveDocs.title: Separator
primitiveDocs.summary: Use separators to divide related content.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Separators expose orientation when semantics are needed.

primitiveDocs.title: Layout
primitiveDocs.summary: Use layouts for full-page shell structure.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Layouts preserve landmarks provided by child header, main, sidebar, and footer regions.

primitiveDocs.title: Container
primitiveDocs.summary: Use containers to constrain readable content width.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Containers are structural and should not replace semantic headings or landmarks.

primitiveDocs.title: Section
primitiveDocs.summary: Use sections to separate meaningful page regions.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Sections should include clear headings when they introduce a new content region.

primitiveDocs.title: Grid
primitiveDocs.summary: Use grids for responsive column layouts.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Grids control layout only; keep reading order meaningful in the source.

primitiveDocs.title: Stack
primitiveDocs.summary: Use stacks for vertical rhythm between related items.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Stacks do not add semantics, so use lists or landmarks when the content requires them.

primitiveDocs.title: Header
primitiveDocs.summary: Use headers for top-level page or section identity.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Headers lower to native header landmarks when used in page structure.

primitiveDocs.title: Footer
primitiveDocs.summary: Use footers for closing page or section metadata.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Footers lower to native footer landmarks when used in page structure.

primitiveDocs.title: Sidebar
primitiveDocs.summary: Use sidebars for static adjacent navigation or secondary content.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Sidebars lower to aside and should be paired with labelled navigation when they contain links.

primitiveDocs.title: Nav
primitiveDocs.summary: Use nav for labelled navigation groups.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Navigation elements must keep clear aria-label values when multiple nav regions are present.

primitiveDocs.title: Breadcrumb
primitiveDocs.summary: Use breadcrumbs to show the current page path.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Breadcrumbs lower to nav and should expose the current page in the trail.

primitiveDocs.title: Image
primitiveDocs.summary: Use images for responsive visual media.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Images preserve platform alt text requirements; decorative images should use an empty alt value.

primitiveDocs.title: Source
primitiveDocs.summary: Use sources inside picture or media elements.
primitiveDocs.accessibility: Sources are media candidates and rely on the parent media element for accessible text.
